options: Compile error — the IOException catch can never run because Exception already catches it
Both catch blocks run, most specific one last
It compiles; whichever catch matches first at runtime wins
It compiles only if readTimetable() declares throws IOException
mcq.explanation_md: Catch blocks are checked **top to bottom**, and `Exception` is the parent of `IOException` — so the first block swallows everything and the second can never execute. Java treats unreachable catch blocks as a **compile error**, not a runtime choice. The tempting wrong answer is the runtime one: the order is fixed at the code level, never decided at runtime. The rule to remember: catch the most specific exception first, the most general last — like sorting complaints to the class rep before escalating to the HOD.
